past_medical_history: COVID-19 vaccination (inactivated virus vaccine, first dose) received approximately 24 hours before symptom onset
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes mellitus, well controlled
Mother healthy, no cardiovascular or neurological disease
Maternal uncle with deep vein thrombosis at age 50, unprovoked

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, less than 1 drink per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and 2-year-old daughter in a ground-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Recreational soccer twice a week, generally active

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but agitated, Glasgow Coma Scale 13 (E3 V5 M5). Oriented to person and place but confused about the date. Able to follow simple one-step commands. Speech is mildly dysarthric. Language comprehension appears grossly intact. Attention is impaired — unable to recite months backward past October.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Visual fields difficult to assess due to agitation but no obvious hemianopia on gross confrontation. No gaze deviation at rest, but subtle preference for rightward gaze noted intermittently. Left-sided lower motor neuron facial palsy with flattening of the left nasolabial fold and inability to fully close the left eye against resistance. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Gag reflex intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Left upper extremity: 0/5 deltoid, 0/5 biceps, 0/5 wrist extension, 0/5 grip strength — complete plegia. Left lower extremity: 0/5 hip flexion, 0/5 knee extension, 0/5 ankle dorsiflexion — complete plegia. Right upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. Left-sided hypotonia noted throughout the left hemibody.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch absent over the left upper and lower extremities. Pain sensation diminished on the left hemibody. Proprioception intact at both great toes. Vibration sensation intact bilaterally at the medial malleoli. Right-sided sensation fully intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+. Left biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+. Left plantar response extensor (Babinski positive). Right plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Cannot be assessed on the left due to complete plegia. Right-sided finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact without dysmetria.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate due to left hemiplegia.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 16. No meningismus. No carotid bruits appreciated. No signs of peripheral embolism. Skin examination unremarkable — no livedo reticularis, petechiae, or injection site abnormality.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden left-sided weakness and facial drooping, preceded by left arm and leg numbness the day before
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Karim Bensaid is a 36-year-old right-handed North African man with no significant past medical history who presents to the emergency department with acute-onset left-sided weakness and facial asymmetry. The patient received his first dose of an inactivated COVID-19 vaccine approximately 48 hours prior to presentation. Approximately 24 hours after vaccination, he noticed a tingling and numbness in his left arm and leg while at work. He attributed this to having slept in an awkward position and did not seek medical attention. He also developed a mild bifrontal headache that he treated with paracetamol 1000 mg. The following morning — approximately 48 hours post-vaccination — his wife found him having difficulty getting out of bed. He was unable to move his left arm or leg and the left side of his face was visibly drooping. She also noted he appeared confused and agitated, which was entirely out of character. Emergency medical services were called and he was transported to the emergency department within 45 minutes of symptom recognition. The patient denies any chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, or visual changes. He reports no fever, neck stiffness, rash, or recent illness prior to vaccination. He has no history of migraines, seizures, or prior neurological symptoms. He has no known history of coagulopathy, autoimmune disease, or cardiac disease. There was no recent long-haul travel, immobilization, or surgery. He denies any use of performance-enhancing substances. His wife reports he has been generally healthy and physically active, playing soccer regularly. The family notes a maternal uncle who had an unprovoked deep vein thrombosis at age 50, but no family history of stroke or arterial thrombotic events. The temporal relationship between vaccination and symptom onset is noted, though the patient and family are uncertain whether this is coincidental.

findings.location: Right deep parietal white matter and cortex, extending into the posterior limb of the right internal capsule
findings.size: Approximately 4.2 x 3.1 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Mild hyperintensity, less conspicuous than DWI abnormality, suggesting relatively early infarct (possibly less than 12-24 hours in evolution)
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity in the right parietal region
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the right parietal region

findings.mass_effect: Mild local sulcal effacement. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Distribution does not conform cleanly to a single major vascular territory — involves both superficial MCA and borderzone territories

findings.location: Within the core of the right parietal infarct

signal_characteristics.SWI: Multiple punctate hypointense foci within the infarct core consistent with petechial hemorrhagic transformation
signal_characteristics.T1: No definite T1 hyperintensity to suggest subacute blood
signal_characteristics.GRE: Punctate susceptibility artifacts in the infarct zone

additional_observations: No evidence of cortical venous thrombosis on standard sequences — dedicated MR venography not performed
MRA of the circle of Willis: bilateral ACA, MCA, and PCA are patent without occlusion or significant stenosis. No large vessel occlusion identified.
MRA of the supra-aortic vessels: bilateral internal carotid arteries and vertebral arteries appear normal in caliber without stenosis, dissection, or occlusion
No prior ischemic changes. No white matter disease.
Normal brain volume for age. No hydrocephalus.
Mild mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus — incidental
mri.impression: Extensive acute right parietal ischemia involving both superficial and deep territories with early petechial hemorrhagic transformation (HI-1 by ECASS classification). No large vessel occlusion on MRA of intracranial and cervical vessels. The distribution does not conform to a typical single-territory arterial infarct and the absence of large vessel occlusion raises concern for embolic etiology (cardiac, paradoxical, or other systemic source). The temporal association with recent vaccination is noted, though radiological features alone cannot establish causation. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: No ST segment elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG. Sinus rhythm at 88 bpm. No abnormalities detected. No evidence of atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other arrhythmia on this tracing.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Glucose 118 mg/dL (H), CRP 6.26 mg/L (H), LDH 256 U/L (H), ESR 18 mm/hr (H).

findings.location: Bilateral internal carotid arteries and vertebral arteries
findings.description: CTA: Bilateral internal carotid arteries are normal in caliber and course without stenosis, dissection, intimal flap, or wall hematoma. Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with normal caliber.

findings.location: Circle of Willis and major branch vessels
findings.description: CTA: Bilateral MCA, ACA, and PCA are patent without occlusion or stenosis. No aneurysm identified. Basilar artery is patent.
CTA: Normal aortic arch morphology (type I). No dissection flap. No aortic atherosclerotic disease. No thrombus identified.

additional_observations: No evidence of arterial dissection in cervical or intracranial vessels
No vasculitis pattern (beading, stenosis-dilation) in intracranial vessels
output.impression: Completely normal CTA of the head and neck. No stenosis, dissection, aneurysm, or vasculitis pattern identified. No aortic arch pathology. The absence of any arterial abnormality in a young patient with extensive right parietal ischemic infarction heightens concern for a cardiac embolic source (PFO with paradoxical embolism, occult arrhythmia, or endocarditis) and warrants urgent echocardiography with bubble study. Clinical correlation recommended.

valves.Aortic Valve: Trileaflet, normal morphology, no stenosis or regurgitation

findings: Interatrial Septum - Bubble Study: Positive for right-to-left shunt with appearance of microbubbles in the left atrium within 3 cardiac cycles after Valsalva release, consistent with patent foramen ovale (PFO)
Vegetations: None identified on any valve
output.impression: Abnormal values: Interatrial Septum - Bubble Study Positive for right-to-left shunt with appearance of microbubbles in the left atrium within 3 cardiac cycles after Valsalva release, consistent with patent foramen ovale (PFO)  (H).

events.description: Normal sinus rhythm throughout the 48-hour recording period (100% of recording)
No atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular arrhythmia detected
Rare premature atrial complexes (42 in 48 hours, <0.1% burden)
Rare premature ventricular complexes (8 in 48 hours)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia

findings: Normal sinus rhythm throughout the 48-hour recording period (100% of recording)
No atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular arrhythmia detected
Rare premature atrial complexes (42 in 48 hours, <0.1% burden)
Rare premature ventricular complexes (8 in 48 hours)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
output.impression: Forty-eight hour continuous Holter monitoring reveals entirely normal sinus rhythm without any arrhythmia. No atrial fibrillation or flutter episodes detected.

findings.region: Bilateral common, internal, and external carotid arteries
findings.signal: Peak_systolic_velocity_right_ICA: 68 cm/s (normal); Peak_systolic_velocity_left_ICA: 72 cm/s (normal); ICA_CCA_ratio_right: 1.2 (normal); ICA_CCA_ratio_left: 1.3 (normal); Plaque: No plaque identified bilaterally

output.impression: Normal carotid duplex ultrasound. No stenosis, plaque, or dissection identified bilaterally. Large artery atherosclerotic disease is excluded as a stroke mechanism. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: COVID-19 vaccine ischemic stroke inactivated virus BIBP Sinopharm vaccine-associated thrombotic events

results.title: Ischemic Stroke After COVID-19 Vaccination: A Case Report
results.authors: El Ouarradi A, Boudhar S, et al.
results.key_finding: Case of a 36-year-old man who developed ischemic stroke 24 hours after first dose of inactivated COVID-19 vaccine (Sinopharm BIBP). Normal vascular imaging and cardiac workup, normal platelets and coagulation. VITT was excluded. Stroke mechanism attributed to possible post-vaccination inflammatory prothrombotic state.

results.title: Cerebrovascular Events After COVID-19 Vaccination: A Systematic Review
results.key_finding: Most vaccine-associated cerebrovascular events are CVST, predominantly with adenoviral vector vaccines (AstraZeneca, J&J). Arterial ischemic strokes are rare and reported with all vaccine types including mRNA and inactivated. Causal relationship remains unproven; the background rate of stroke may explain temporal associations.

results.title: VITT vs Non-VITT Thrombotic Events After COVID-19 Vaccination
results.authors: Pavord S, Scully M, et al.
results.key_finding: VITT is characterized by thrombocytopenia, elevated D-dimer, positive anti-PF4 antibodies, and occurs 5-30 days post adenoviral vector vaccination. Patients with normal platelets, normal D-dimer, and negative anti-PF4 do not have VITT. Inactivated virus vaccines have not been associated with VITT.

output.summary: The literature on post-COVID-19 vaccination ischemic stroke shows that the vast majority of vaccine-associated thrombotic events are cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) linked to adenoviral vector vaccines via the VITT mechanism (anti-PF4 antibodies, thrombocytopenia). Arterial ischemic stroke is rarely reported and occurs with all vaccine types. In cases with normal platelet count, normal D-dimer, negative anti-PF4 antibodies, and normal coagulation profile — as in this patient — VITT is effectively excluded. A direct causal link between inactivated COVID-19 vaccines and arterial ischemic stroke has not been established, and temporal association may be coincidental. When traditional risk factors and VITT are excluded, thorough investigation for alternative etiologies (PFO, thrombophilia, occult arrhythmia, vasculitis) should be pursued rather than attributing the stroke solely to vaccination.

output.query: patent foramen ovale cryptogenic stroke young adults PFO closure paradoxical embolism management

results.title: Patent Foramen Ovale Closure or Anticoagulation vs. Antiplatelets after Stroke (CLOSE Trial)
results.authors: Mas JL, Derumeaux G, et al.
results.key_finding: PFO closure with antiplatelet therapy was superior to antiplatelet therapy alone in preventing recurrent stroke in patients aged 16-60 with cryptogenic stroke and PFO with atrial septal aneurysm or large shunt. NNT of 20 over 5 years.

results.title: Long-Term Outcomes of Patent Foramen Ovale Closure (RESPECT Extended)
results.authors: Saver JL, Carroll JD, et al.
results.key_finding: PFO closure with the Amplatzer device reduced the risk of recurrent ischemic stroke in patients with cryptogenic stroke and PFO (HR 0.38, 95% CI 0.18-0.79).

results.title: AHA/ASA Guidelines: PFO Management in Cryptogenic Stroke
results.authors: Messé SR, Gronseth GS, et al.
results.key_finding: For patients aged 18-60 with cryptogenic stroke and PFO, closure may be considered particularly when high-risk PFO features are present (large shunt, atrial septal aneurysm, or hypermobility). The RoPE score can help estimate the probability that PFO is stroke-related.

output.summary: In young patients (age <60) with cryptogenic ischemic stroke and PFO, current evidence from CLOSE, RESPECT, and REDUCE trials supports PFO closure as superior to antiplatelet therapy alone for secondary stroke prevention, particularly when high-risk PFO features are present. The RoPE score (based on age, absence of risk factors, cortical infarct on imaging, and other factors) helps estimate the likelihood that the PFO is pathologically related to the stroke — younger patients with no traditional risk factors and cortical infarcts have the highest scores. For this patient (age 36, no risk factors, cortical infarct, PFO with shunt), the RoPE score would be high, strongly supporting a causative role of the PFO and favoring closure.

output.proposed: Aspirin 100 mg once daily + Clopidogrel 75 mg once daily (dual antiplatelet therapy)
interactions: No current medications to interact with — patient was on no chronic medications prior to admission
contraindications: No absolute contraindications identified. Normal renal and hepatic function. No history of GI bleeding or peptic ulcer disease.
warnings: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) increases bleeding risk compared to single antiplatelet therapy
DAPT is typically used for 1-3 months post-stroke in the acute/subacute phase, then stepped down to single agent
If PFO closure is pursued, DAPT is typically continued for 3-6 months post-procedure followed by aspirin monotherapy
Consider PPI (e.g., pantoprazole) for GI protection during DAPT
alternatives: Aspirin monotherapy 100 mg daily (lower efficacy for secondary prevention in first 90 days)
Anticoagulation with apixaban 5 mg BID (may be considered if PFO with atrial septal aneurysm, though data favor closure over anticoagulation)
Ticagrelor 90 mg BID + aspirin (not standard for stroke; primarily used in acute coronary syndromes)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right parietal territory, cryptogenic — most likely due to paradoxical embolism through patent foramen ovale (PFO) in a young patient with temporal association to recent COVID-19 vaccination

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal platelet count, normal D-dimer, normal fibrinogen, negative anti-PF4 antibodies. VITT is primarily associated with adenoviral vector vaccines, not inactivated virus vaccines.

differential.diagnosis: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with venous infarction
differential.key_distinguishing: Normal MR venography would exclude this. DWI restriction pattern and arterial territory distribution favor arterial ischemia. No papilledema or raised ICP features.
Afebrile, no murmur, negative blood cultures, no vegetations on echocardiography. Normal inflammatory markers apart from mildly elevated CRP.
Normal CTA and MRA of cervical vessels without dissection flap, intimal irregularity, or wall hematoma. No neck pain or Horner syndrome.
Negative anticardiolipin antibodies, negative beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies, negative lupus anticoagulant. Would need repeat testing at 12 weeks for definitive exclusion.
MRI confirms ischemic infarction with DWI restriction. Petechial hemorrhagic change is secondary transformation of ischemic infarct, not primary hemorrhagic stroke.

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code, perform NIHSS assessment, establish symptom onset timeline relative to vaccination, and obtain fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 16, symptom onset 24h after vaccination with prodrome of numbness, glucose mildly elevated

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage, followed by brain MRI with DWI/FLAIR/SWI to characterize the infarct
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right parietal ischemic infarct with early hemorrhagic transformation; no large vessel occlusion on MRA; atypical territory raising suspicion for embolic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC with differential, BMP, coagulation panel with fibrinogen and D-dimer, inflammatory markers (CRP, procalcitonin), lipid panel, and HbA1c
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC with normal platelets (excluding VITT), normal coagulation, mildly elevated CRP, normal metabolic panel — no traditional risk factors

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia as embolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm — no arrhythmia, cryptogenic stroke

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel disease, dissection, or vasculitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Completely normal cervical and intracranial vasculature — no stenosis, dissection, or vasculitis

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram with agitated saline bubble study to evaluate for PFO and cardiac source of embolism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Structurally normal heart with PFO demonstrated on bubble study — critical finding establishing stroke mechanism

optimal_actions.action: Order hypercoagulability panel including antiphospholipid antibodies and anti-PF4 antibodies (VITT screen)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal thrombophilia workup and negative anti-PF4 — definitively excluding VITT and hereditary thrombophilia

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous cardiac telemetry and prolonged Holter monitoring to exclude paroxysmal arrhythmia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm throughout — arrhythmia excluded

optimal_actions.action: Admit to stroke unit, initiate antiplatelet therapy (aspirin), and manage blood pressure with permissive hypertension in the acute phase
After PFO is identified and VITT/thrombophilia excluded, refer for PFO closure consultation and initiate dual antiplatelet therapy as bridge
Search literature on vaccine-associated stroke and PFO-related cryptogenic stroke management to inform clinical decision-making
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence that VITT is associated with adenoviral vector vaccines, not inactivated; PFO closure trials support closure in young cryptogenic stroke patients

critical_actions: Recognize that normal platelet count and normal D-dimer effectively exclude VITT, despite temporal association with vaccination
Do not attribute the stroke solely to vaccination without exhaustive workup for alternative etiologies
Order echocardiogram with bubble study to detect PFO as a potential mechanism for paradoxical embolism
Complete hypercoagulability workup in a young patient with no traditional vascular risk factors
Order vascular imaging (CTA or MRA) to exclude dissection, which is a common cause of stroke in young adults
Recognize that the atypical infarct territory and absence of large vessel disease point toward an embolic mechanism
Do not administer IV tPA if hemorrhagic transformation is already present on initial imaging
contraindicated_actions: Administering IV alteplase in the presence of early hemorrhagic transformation visible on initial MRI
Attributing the stroke to vaccination and stopping the workup without evaluating for PFO, dissection, thrombophilia, and arrhythmia
Diagnosing VITT without thrombocytopenia or positive anti-PF4 antibodies — treating empirically with non-heparin anticoagulants and IVIG for presumed VITT would be inappropriate
Initiating full-dose anticoagulation in the acute phase with hemorrhagic transformation present — risk of expansion
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute phase with large ischemic territory
Dismissing the post-vaccination inflammatory markers (CRP, LDH, ESR) as unrelated without considering their potential contribution to a prothrombotic state
key_reasoning_points: The temporal association between COVID-19 vaccination and stroke onset is a red herring that may lead the clinician to prematurely diagnose VITT — however, normal platelets, normal D-dimer, and normal coagulation definitively exclude VITT
VITT is primarily associated with adenoviral vector vaccines (AstraZeneca, Johnson & Johnson), not inactivated virus vaccines like Sinopharm BIBP
The absence of any vascular abnormality on CTA/MRA in a young patient with a large infarct should trigger aggressive cardiac workup, particularly echocardiography with bubble study for PFO

Patent foramen ovale is present in approximately 25% of the general population, but its prevalence in young cryptogenic stroke patients is 40-50%, strongly implicating paradoxical embolism as the mechanism

The mildly elevated CRP and inflammatory markers may reflect post-vaccination inflammation that could contribute to a transient prothrombotic state, potentially facilitating thrombus formation and paradoxical embolism through a PFO
Early petechial hemorrhagic transformation (HI-1 by ECASS) is common in embolic strokes due to reperfusion and is a contraindication to thrombolysis but not to antiplatelet therapy

The diagnostic puzzle requires the agent to systematically exclude VITT, dissection, atherosclerosis, arrhythmia, and thrombophilia before arriving at PFO-mediated paradoxical embolism as the most likely mechanism

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle — young patient with no traditional risk factors presenting with acute ischemic stroke 24 hours after COVID-19 vaccination. The temporal association creates a strong cognitive anchor toward VITT, but normal platelets and coagulation exclude this diagnosis. The true mechanism (paradoxical embolism through PFO) is only revealed through systematic workup including echocardiography with bubble study. Multiple red herrings including vaccination timing, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, and hemorrhagic transformation complicate the diagnostic reasoning.
clinical_pearls: VITT requires thrombocytopenia and positive anti-PF4 antibodies — do not diagnose it based on temporal association with vaccination alone
Inactivated virus vaccines (Sinopharm, Sinovac) have not been associated with VITT, which is linked to adenoviral vector vaccines
PFO should be suspected in any young cryptogenic stroke patient, especially when vascular imaging is normal — always request a bubble study
Post-vaccination inflammatory state may contribute to a transient prothrombotic milieu, but this alone does not establish causation for stroke
The RoPE score helps quantify the probability that a PFO is stroke-related rather than incidental
